title: At the Restaurant
description: Order food, ask about dishes, and pay the bill in a Filipino carinderia or restaurant.
situation: Eating out: getting a table, ordering, asking what's good, and settling the bill.

phrases.tip: Swap dalawa for any number: isa (one), tatlo (three).

phrases.native: Pwede po bang makita ang menu?
phrases.english: Can I see the menu, please?
phrases.pronunciation: PWE-de po bang ma-KEE-ta ang me-NOO

phrases.tip: A great way to discover local specialties.
Adobo is the classic soy-and-vinegar stew, a national favourite.

phrases.native: Hindi po ako kumakain ng baboy
phrases.pronunciation: hin-DEE po a-KO koo-ma-KA-in nang BA-boy
phrases.tip: Useful in a country that loves lechon (roast pig).
Rice (kanin) is the heart of nearly every Filipino meal.
Mixing in English words (called Taglish) is completely normal.
Cooks love to hear this. Add talaga (really) for emphasis.
The paki- prefix turns any verb into a polite request.
You can also wave and ask for the chit.
Many small eateries are cash-only, so it's worth asking first.
Honesty about change is appreciated and very common.
